chapters.title: Subjunctive & Polite Requests
chapters.description: Use the subjunctive mood for wishes, doubts, and polite requests.

lessons.prompt: What does the Hindi subjunctive form express?
lessons.explanation: Subjunctive (sambhavana) expresses possibility, desire, or softened commands. 'Woh aaye' = Let him come / May he come / He might come.

lessons.prompt: शायद वह कल ___। (Perhaps she will come tomorrow.)
lessons.explanation: 'Shayad' (perhaps) triggers subjunctive. 'Aaye' = subjunctive of aana. Not 'aayegi' (definite future).

lessons.prompt: Translate politely: 'Please sit down' (very formal)
lessons.hint: 'Baithiye' is the formal imperative (aap form). 'Baitho' = informal, 'baithe' = subjunctive/soft request.

lessons.prompt: What particle often accompanies subjunctive to mean 'so that'?
lessons.explanation: 'Taaki' = so that (triggers subjunctive in the purpose clause). 'Main uthha taaki woh baith sake' = I got up so that she could sit.

lessons.prompt: काश! मेरे पास ज़्यादा वक्त ___। (If only I had more time!)
lessons.explanation: 'Kaash' = if only / I wish. Always followed by subjunctive/counterfactual. 'Hota' = were (counterfactual).

learningMaterial.title: Subjunctive & Polite Requests
learningMaterial.intro: Use the subjunctive mood for wishes, doubts, and polite requests.

concepts.explanation: Subjunctive (sambhavana) expresses possibility, desire, or softened commands. 'Woh aaye' = Let him come / May he come / He might come.
'Shayad' (perhaps) triggers subjunctive. 'Aaye' = subjunctive of aana. Not 'aayegi' (definite future).
'Baithiye' is the formal imperative (aap form). 'Baitho' = informal, 'baithe' = subjunctive/soft request.
'Taaki' = so that (triggers subjunctive in the purpose clause). 'Main uthha taaki woh baith sake' = I got up so that she could sit.
'Kaash' = if only / I wish. Always followed by subjunctive/counterfactual. 'Hota' = were (counterfactual).

chapters.title: Hindi Idioms & Proverbs
chapters.description: Master common Hindi idioms and proverbs used by native speakers.

lessons.prompt: What does 'आँखें चार होना (aankhein chaar hona)' mean?
options: To fall in love / to meet eyes
lessons.correctAnswer: To fall in love / to meet eyes
lessons.explanation: Literally 'for eyes to become four' — to make eye contact, often implying falling in love or mutual recognition.

lessons.prompt: नाच न जाने आँगन ___। (A bad workman blames his tools — lit: can't dance, blames the courtyard)
lessons.explanation: 'Naach na jaane aangan tedha' = Can't dance (but says) the courtyard is crooked. Equivalent of 'a bad workman blames his tools'.

lessons.prompt: What does 'दूध का दूध पानी का पानी करना' mean?
lessons.correctAnswer: to get to the truth / separate milk from water
lessons.hint: Literally 'make milk milk and water water' — to separate truth from falsehood, to give a just verdict.

lessons.prompt: What does 'हाथ पाँव मारना (haath paanv maarna)' mean?
lessons.explanation: Literally 'to strike hands and feet' — to make efforts, to struggle, to try hard to achieve something.

lessons.prompt: अब पछताए ___ क्या जब चिड़िया चुग गई खेत।(What's the use of regret after the damage is done?)
lessons.explanation: Famous proverb: 'Ab pachtaye hot kya jab chidiya chug gayi khet' = No use crying over spilt milk. Lit: What's the point of regret when birds already ate the field.

learningMaterial.title: Hindi Idioms & Proverbs
learningMaterial.intro: Master common Hindi idioms and proverbs used by native speakers.

concepts.term: To fall in love / to meet eyes
concepts.explanation: Literally 'for eyes to become four' — to make eye contact, often implying falling in love or mutual recognition.
'Naach na jaane aangan tedha' = Can't dance (but says) the courtyard is crooked. Equivalent of 'a bad workman blames his tools'.

concepts.term: to get to the truth / separate milk from water
concepts.explanation: Literally 'make milk milk and water water' — to separate truth from falsehood, to give a just verdict.
Literally 'to strike hands and feet' — to make efforts, to struggle, to try hard to achieve something.
Famous proverb: 'Ab pachtaye hot kya jab chidiya chug gayi khet' = No use crying over spilt milk. Lit: What's the point of regret when birds already ate the field.

chapters.title: Formal Hindi & Register
chapters.description: Write and speak in formal Hindi for professional and official contexts.

lessons.prompt: What is the formal Hindi word for 'please' in official letters?
lessons.explanation: 'Kripaya' (कृपया) = please (formal). Used in official communication, letters, and signs. More formal than 'please' borrowed from English.

lessons.prompt: आपका ___ पत्र प्राप्त हुआ। (Your letter has been received — formal)
lessons.explanation: Formal letter opening. 'Aapka patr prapt hua' = Your letter has been received. Official Hindi letters use Sanskritized vocabulary.

lessons.prompt: Translate formally: 'I request your attention to this matter'
lessons.correctAnswer: मैं इस विषय पर आपका ध्यान आकर्षित करना चाहता हूँ
lessons.hint: 'Dhyan aakarshit karna' = to attract attention. 'Vishay' = matter/subject. Formal request structure.

lessons.prompt: Which formal term is used for 'government' in official Hindi?
lessons.explanation: 'Shasaan' (शासन) = governance/administration (most formal). 'Sarkar' (सरकार) is common formal. Used in official documents and news.

lessons.prompt: इस ___ के संदर्भ में आपसे अनुरोध है। (With reference to this matter, you are requested...)
lessons.explanation: 'Vishay' (विषय) = subject/matter. 'Sandarbh mein' = with reference to. 'Anurodh hai' = it is requested. Classic formal Hindi structure.

learningMaterial.title: Formal Hindi & Register
learningMaterial.intro: Write and speak in formal Hindi for professional and official contexts.

concepts.explanation: 'Kripaya' (कृपया) = please (formal). Used in official communication, letters, and signs. More formal than 'please' borrowed from English.
Formal letter opening. 'Aapka patr prapt hua' = Your letter has been received. Official Hindi letters use Sanskritized vocabulary.

concepts.term: मैं इस विषय पर आपका ध्यान आकर्षित करना चाहता हूँ
concepts.explanation: 'Dhyan aakarshit karna' = to attract attention. 'Vishay' = matter/subject. Formal request structure.
'Shasaan' (शासन) = governance/administration (most formal). 'Sarkar' (सरकार) is common formal. Used in official documents and news.
'Vishay' (विषय) = subject/matter. 'Sandarbh mein' = with reference to. 'Anurodh hai' = it is requested. Classic formal Hindi structure.

chapters.title: Compound Verbs & Nuance
chapters.description: Master compound verb constructions that give Hindi its expressive nuance.

lessons.prompt: What nuance does 'khaana kha lena' add vs just 'khaana khaana'?
lessons.explanation: 'Le lena' = complete the action (for own benefit/finality). 'Kha liya' = ate up (done). 'Le' adds a sense of completion.

lessons.prompt: वह रो ___। (She burst out crying — sudden, uncontrolled action)
lessons.explanation: 'Padna' as compound verb = sudden/involuntary action. 'Ro padi' = burst into tears. 'Has para' = burst out laughing.

lessons.prompt: Translate with appropriate compound verb: 'Finish your homework completely'
lessons.hint: 'Dalna' as compound = do thoroughly/get it done (with some force). 'Kar daalo' = finish it off.

lessons.prompt: What does 'jaana' add as a compound verb after another verb?
lessons.explanation: 'Jaana' as compound = action is complete and irreversible. 'Mar gaya' = died (gone). 'Kho gaya' = lost (gone forever).

lessons.prompt: मैंने किताब पढ़ ___। (I finished reading the book completely.)
lessons.explanation: 'Le lena' = complete for self. 'Padh li' = read it (completely, for myself). Past feminine because 'kitaab' is feminine.

learningMaterial.title: Compound Verbs & Nuance
learningMaterial.intro: Master compound verb constructions that give Hindi its expressive nuance.

concepts.explanation: 'Le lena' = complete the action (for own benefit/finality). 'Kha liya' = ate up (done). 'Le' adds a sense of completion.
'Padna' as compound verb = sudden/involuntary action. 'Ro padi' = burst into tears. 'Has para' = burst out laughing.
'Dalna' as compound = do thoroughly/get it done (with some force). 'Kar daalo' = finish it off.
'Jaana' as compound = action is complete and irreversible. 'Mar gaya' = died (gone). 'Kho gaya' = lost (gone forever).
'Le lena' = complete for self. 'Padh li' = read it (completely, for myself). Past feminine because 'kitaab' is feminine.
